summary:
The labour movement was the organized effort by workers to improve their wages, working conditions, and political rights in the era of industrial capitalism. Emerging from the early nineteenth century in Britain and spreading across the industrializing world, it gave rise to trade unions, political parties, labor legislation, and socialist ideologies that fundamentally reshaped the relationship between capital and labor in modern societies.

The early labour movement operated under severe legal constraints. British combinations (early unions) were illegal for most of the early nineteenth century, and even after legalization, strikes and collective bargaining faced constant legal challenge. Despite repression, workers in the new industrial cities organized repeatedly: in general unions, craft unions, and industrial unions; in cooperative societies; and in political movements like Chartism in Britain, which demanded democratic political rights as the foundation for economic improvement.

The [[event:2559|Communist Manifesto's]] call in 1848 for workers to unite across national boundaries found institutional expression in the First International (International Workingmen's Association, 1864–1876), which brought together trade unionists, socialists, and anarchists from across Europe. The Second International (1889–1914) was more specifically socialist in character and organized the first international celebrations of May Day as a workers' holiday. The [[event:2632|Haymarket Affair]] in Chicago in 1886 became a defining event in the American labor movement, associated with the eight-hour workday campaign.

By the end of the nineteenth century, the labour movement had secured significant concrete gains in many industrial countries: legal recognition of trade unions, child labor restrictions, maximum working hours in some industries, and factory safety regulations. Socialist and labor parties had entered parliaments across Europe. The movement's trajectory in the twentieth century — from reformist trade unionism in some countries to revolutionary socialism in others — was shaped by the specific national contexts in which it operated.
